content:
You are a creative and professional JavaScript game developer who builds polished and visually appealing video games using p5.js primitives.
Your game should offer a fun and engaging game experience for the player following game development principles.
Create a learnable progression ramp that balances learning, fun, and challenge.
Use your creativity to enrich the concept into an innovative design before coding, adding interesting and consistent elements beyond the game concept.
Ensure the implementation is free of inconsistencies and errors. Write modular ES6 code with automated tests covering key aspects of the game.
Follow the hard constraints exactly; violating any is a critical error.

<hard_constraints>
- The full game can be expanded by adding more elements to the game concept but should align with the game concept respecting the hard constraints and game design principles.
- Game must load and function without errors, start on pressing ENTER, gameplay must be responsive to player inputs, and have correct game phase handling.
- Game phases: START→PLAYING (ENTER), PLAYING→GAME_OVER (win/lose), GAME_OVER→START (R), PLAYING↔PAUSED (ESC). Expose this in a variable called `gameState.gamePhase` where `gameState` is a global object accessible via `window.getGameState()`.
- Start screen should have a title, short description of the game, instructions to play the game, and a "PRESS ENTER TO START" prompt.
- Allowed libraries: p5.js, p5.collide2D
- Canvas: `600 × 400 px`; target 60 FPS
- Keyboard inputs only. No mouse control.
- Allowed gameplay control keys: Arrow keys (37-40), Space (32), Shift (16), Z (90)
- Game phase specific controls:
    - ENTER (13)  – start the game
    - ESC (27)    – pause / unpause
    - R (82)      – restart; returns to the start screen, where the player can press ENTER to play again
- No external images, sprites, fonts, or other assets. Make visual assets and animations using code.
- No audio or sound effects.
- Use p5.js in instance mode and expose the game instance globally as window.gameInstance.
- Ensure game reproducibility using `p.randomSeed(42)` in the `setup` function. No other random seeding.
- Use ES6 syntax with proper imports and exports at the top of the file where they are used. No dynamic imports or require() imports.
- Maintain `p.logs` as write-only; log game_info, inputs, player_info with proper initialization and updates during the game loop.
</hard_constraints>

<instructions>
<game_concept_to_game_design>
Expand the game concept with new game elements with a well-planned progression and learning curve that enables the player to learn about the game elements and mechanics.
For inspiration, following are some examples of game elements that you can add to the game design as long as the final game satisfies the requirements of the game concept:
- environment: game setting, genre, viewpoint
- entities: characters, opponents, collaborators, NPCs, objects in the game, power-ups, destructible objects, obstacles
- mechanics: rules of the games, final win conditions, game over conditions, subgoals, rewards, player actions and abilities, interaction between entities
- graphics: visual appearance and animations of the game entities and the environment
</game_concept_to_game_design>

<code_instructions>
You are encouraged to write as much code as you can to make a fun game, aesthetically appealing, and provide a fun gameplay experience for the player when playing the game for the first time.

<code_organization>
- Modular code organization with proper ES6 imports:
  - Organize the code into multiple files as needed (game.js, globals.js, etc.)
  - Group related functionality together in meaningful modules
  - Proper imports:
    - Use ES6 for imports and exports
    - Every file MUST import ALL constants, variables, functions, classes, and external symbols at the top of every file. No Node.js style require imports. No dynamic imports within functions
```javascript
// Essential pattern (ALWAYS follow this)
import { Player, Enemy } from './entities.js';
import { checkCollision, applyPhysics } from './physics.js';
import { gameState, CANVAS_WIDTH } from './globals.js';

// Example function with proper dependencies
function createPlayer(x, y) {
  const player = new Player(x, y);
  gameState.player = player;
  gameState.entities.push(player);
  return player;
}
```

- State Management:
  - Implement `function getGameState()` and attach it to `window`. It must return the `gameState` object.
  - Use a single `gameState` object to track the game's current data and status. Store all game data in the `gameState` object to be used by automated testing code to play-test the game.    
```javascript
export const gameState = {
  player: null,       // player entity to be initialized in the setup function and initialized before the game starts
  entities: [],       // all game entities including player
  score: 0,
  gamePhase: "START", // "START", "PLAYING", "GAME_OVER_WIN/LOSE", "PAUSED"
  controlMode: "HUMAN", // "HUMAN", "TEST_1", "TEST_2", "TEST_3", etc.
  ... // other game state variables which are needed to be tracked for automated testing
};
```
</code_organization>

<p5_instructions>
- Use p5.js in instance mode and store the p5 instance in a variable called `gameInstance`. Expose the game instance globally as follows:
```javascript
const p5 = window.p5
let gameInstance = new p5(p => {
    // Initialize variables
    ...
    // Initialize the logs. Important: Do not reset the logs at any point in the code! logs are considered write-only!
    p.logs = {
        "game_info": [],  // Information about the game
        "inputs": [],     // Information about the key inputs
        "player_info": [] // Information about the player character
    };

    // Functions
    ...
});
// Expose the game instance globally
window.gameInstance = gameInstance;
```
- p.logs is write-only. Do not reset it. We want to keep track of all the logs since the start of the p5.js game instance.
- Variables to store in the p.logs during gameplay:
  - In "game_info": Information about the game when there are changes in the game phases
    - "data": Any data like game phase or anything else you want to log
    - "framecount" and "timestamp": The framecount and timestamp of the event accessed using `p.frameCount` and `Date.now()` respectively
  - In "inputs": Store the control inputs when they are triggered by the player
    - "input_type": The input event type (keyPressed, keyReleased, etc.)
    - "data": Additional data specific to the input type. Store `key` and `keyCode` if the input is a key.
    - "framecount" and "timestamp": The framecount and timestamp of the event
  - In "player_info": Information about the player character when there are changes in the player's state (accessed via `gameState.player` where `gameState` is accessible via `window.getGameState()`)
    - "screen_x", "screen_y": The x and y position of the player on the screen
    - "game_x", "game_y": The x and y position of the player in the game world
    - "framecount": The framecount of the event

- Useful Functions:
  - Use p. to call p5 functions.
  - p5: `keyIsDown(key_code)`, `noLoop()/loop()`, `tint()`, `push()/pop()`, `textAlign()`, `textSize()`, `text()`
  - p5.collide2d for collision detection
    - Note that the specific order of the shapes in the function name matters. For example, 'collideCircleRect' is not available.
    - Available functions: `collidePointPoint`, `collidePointCircle`, `collidePointEllipse`, `collidePointRect`, `collidePointLine`, `collidePointArc`, `collideRectRect`, `collideCircleCircle`, `collideRectCircle`, `collideLineLine`, `collideLineCircle`, `collideLineRect`, `collidePointPoly`, `collideCirclePoly`, `collideRectPoly`, `collideLinePoly`, `collidePolyPoly`, `collidePointTriangle`
</p5_instructions>

<game_mechanics_instructions>
Implement intuitive, believable mechanics that enable players to achieve the final goal.
Choose parameters and logic for game mechanics that are intuitive and believable. These can change as the game progresses. 
Even at the end of the game, the mechanics must be such that the player can win by showing their learned skill.
- Controls:
  - Player controls must be intuitive and impact the game state in a meaningful way
  - Input handling: Use keyboard for HUMAN mode, automated testing action outputs for automated testing modes
  - When automated testing mode is selected, the game must automatically play using the action outputs from the automated testing controller
- Non-player characters and objects:
  - NPCs and objects must be spawned and behave respecting the progression of the game
  - Implement intelligent, intuitive, and challenging behavior for all game entities to keep the player engaged
- Interactions with other entities and objects:
  - Have plausible physics with parameters and logic to give it a believable realistic feel
  - Prevent objects from passing through each other or behaving inconsistently to prevent shocking the player
- GAME_OVER Conditions:
  - Implement mechanics that can lead to win or lose conditions achievable by the player
  - GAME_OVER must be a final state followed by game over screen
- Rewards:
  - Define meaningful and reasonable conditions for rewards to keep the player motivated to reach the final goal
  - Do not excessively repeat the same rewards
</game_mechanics_instructions>

<rendering_instructions>
- Implement aesthetic objects, smooth animations, and dynamic effects for the game environment, entities, and their interactions
- Provide useful visual feedback:
  - Player character must be distinctly identifiable
  - Provide visual feedback for interactions and response to their actions
  - UI: Add well-positioned text and graphics to inform the player about information like score, health, etc.
  - Never display current controlMode (HUMAN/TESTING) on the game canvas.
- Ensure the correct render order of the game objects to ensure player is visible on the screen
- Avoid flickering:
  - Do not call p.randomSeed() inside draw()
  - Have exactly one call to p.background() at the top of draw()
  - Do not draw elements that are randomly sampled at every frame as this causes flickering
  - Use ternary operator to set colors for conditional rendering: Example: `p.fill(...(CONDITION ? [255, 220, 150] : [40, 30, 20]));`
- Required Informative Game Screens:
  - Start: Title, clear instructions, objectives, and controls with actions for the player, "PRESS ENTER TO START"
  - Playing: Game rendering, score, and active gameplay
  - Pause: Small text "PAUSED" indicator in top right
  - Game Over: Win/Lose message, final score, "PRESS R TO RESTART", R takes you back to the start screen
</rendering_instructions>

</code_instructions>
</instructions>


<task>
Implement a complete, fun, and error-free p5.js game for the following concept:
{
  "original_game_name": "Race Master 3D: Car Racing",
  "game_name": "Pixel Drift Race",
  "genre": "Arcade Racing",
  "core_mechanics": "The player controls a single car on a vertically scrolling, multi-lane track from a top-down perspective. The car accelerates continuously while the 'accelerate' key is held, and naturally decelerates when released. Steering involves moving the car laterally across lanes. Colliding with obstacles or rival cars reduces the car's health. Initiating a drift by holding the 'hard brake/drift' key while turning provides a temporary speed boost and potential point bonuses. Cash is earned by completing levels and taking risks (like high-speed overtakes), which can be used to upgrade car stats (Engine, Acceleration, Handling) or customize its appearance. The primary objective is to complete levels, defeat boss cars, and manage car health to avoid game over.",
  "visual_elements": "The game is rendered in a top-down 2D style. \n- **Player Car**: A simple rectangular polygon (e.g., a green rectangle for the body, smaller grey rectangles for wheels) with a distinct primary color and an optional secondary color for customization. Slight sprite lean during turns. Subtle exhaust particle effect for acceleration. Red taillights flash during braking.\n- **Rival Cars**: Similar rectangular polygons to the player car, but in different, less prominent colors (e.g., shades of blue, red, yellow).\n- **Boss Cars**: Larger, more detailed polygonal shapes with unique visual identifiers (e.g., jagged edges, distinct color scheme, glowing elements). May emit special particles for attacks.\n- **Obstacles**: \n    - **Static**: Potholes (dark grey circles), road barriers (short, horizontal grey/yellow striped rectangles), oil slicks (dark, shiny puddles).\n    - **Dynamic**: Moving road blocks (small rectangular objects that slowly traverse lanes), ramps (wedge-shaped grey polygons for elevation changes).\n- **Track**: The road surface is a solid dark grey or asphalt texture. Lane dividers are dashed white lines. The background changes per location (e.g., green for suburban, grey buildings for city, brown/rocky for mountain pass) and scrolls vertically to simulate movement.\n- **UI Elements**: \n    - **Score**: Top-left, 'SCORE: [value]'.\n    - **Level**: Top-right, 'LEVEL: [number]'.\n    - **Cash**: Bottom-left, 'CASH: $[value]'.\n    - **Health Bar**: Below the car, a horizontal green bar that depletes to red.\n    - **Speed Indicator**: A simple speedometer graphic or text value (e.g., 'MPH: [value]') on the bottom-right.\n    - **Menus**: Main Menu, Pause Menu, Level Complete, Game Over screens with clear buttons and text.\n- **Animations/Effects**: \n    - **Car Movement**: Smooth vertical scrolling of the track, smooth lateral car movement. Car sprite slightly rotates/tilts during turns. \n    - **Drift**: Fading tire mark trails (white/grey lines) behind the car, subtle dust/smoke particles, a brief 'speed lines' effect at the car's rear.\n    - **Collision**: Player car flashes briefly, small impact particle effect (stars/sparks), slight car shake.\n    - **Explosion (Game Over)**: Car breaks into a few larger debris pieces and many small particle explosions.\n    - **Power-up (if implemented later)**: Glows, then disappears with a small animation upon collection.\n    - **Level Transition**: Full-screen overlay with 'LEVEL X COMPLETE!' text, fading in and out.\n    - **Boss Battle**: Boss car features attack animations (e.g., shooting projectiles, glowing for special abilities).",
  "control_scheme": "The control scheme maps the original mobile touch input to standard keyboard keys for precise arcade control:\n- **`ArrowUp`** or **`W`**: Accelerate (Hold down to continuously increase speed. Releasing allows natural deceleration/braking).\n- **`ArrowLeft`** or **`A`**: Steer Left (Moves the car one lane left or shifts position within the lane).\n- **`ArrowRight`** or **`D`**: Steer Right (Moves the car one lane right or shifts position within the lane).\n- **`Space`**: Hard Brake / Initiate Drift (Hold while turning left or right to execute a sharper drift maneuver, which can provide a temporary speed boost or point bonus if executed correctly).\n- **`P`** (not on list, but standard for pause functionality): Pause/Resume Game.\n\nThere are no special key combinations beyond holding 'Space' while steering for a drift.",
  "game_states": "The game flow is managed through distinct states:\n- **`LOADING`**: Initial state where game assets (images, sounds, fonts) are loaded. Transitions to `MAIN_MENU` once loaded.\n- **`MAIN_MENU`**: Displays game title, 'START RACE', 'GARAGE', 'HIGHSCORES', 'OPTIONS' buttons. Selecting 'START RACE' transitions to `LEVEL_SELECT`. Selecting 'GARAGE' transitions to `GARAGE_MENU`.\n- **`LEVEL_SELECT`**: Shows available levels, current progress, and 'BACK' button. Selecting an unlocked level transitions to `PLAYING` for that level.\n- **`GARAGE_MENU`**: Allows player to spend cash on car upgrades (Engine, Acceleration, Handling) or customizations (paint jobs). Displays current cash and car stats. Has a 'BACK' button to return to `MAIN_MENU` or `LEVEL_SELECT`.\n- **`PLAYING`**: The active racing state. Player controls the car, track scrolls, obstacles appear. Transitions to `PAUSED` on pause input, `LEVEL_COMPLETE` on victory conditions, or `GAME_OVER` on loss conditions.\n- **`PAUSED`**: Game logic and rendering are frozen. Displays 'PAUSED' message and 'RESUME', 'RESTART', 'MAIN MENU' options. Selecting 'RESUME' returns to `PLAYING`.\n- **`LEVEL_COMPLETE`**: Displays 'LEVEL X COMPLETE!' (or 'BOSS DEFEATED!'), level score, cash earned, and 'NEXT LEVEL' / 'LEVEL SELECT' buttons. Transitions to `LEVEL_SELECT` or directly to the next level's `PLAYING` state if applicable.\n- **`GAME_OVER`**: Displays 'GAME OVER', total score, and 'RETRY' / 'MAIN MENU' buttons. Transitions to `MAIN_MENU` or `PLAYING` (for retry).\n- **`WIN_GAME`**: Special state triggered upon completing the final level and defeating the ultimate boss. Displays 'YOU WIN!', final score, and 'MAIN MENU' button. Transitions to `MAIN_MENU`.\n\nState transitions are triggered by player input (button clicks, key presses) or internal game logic (health reaches zero, finish line crossed, boss defeated).",
  "win_conditions": "The player wins a single level by reaching the finish line with car health greater than 0, potentially within a given time limit (for race levels), or by fully depleting the health of a boss car (for boss levels). The player wins the entire game by successfully completing the final designated level (Level 4, 'Boss Battle: The Enforcer' in this specification) under the aforementioned conditions.",
  "lose_conditions": "The player loses a level (and the game if no retries) under the following conditions:\n- **Car Health Depletion**: Player car's health reaches 0 due to collisions with rivals or obstacles.\n- **Time Out**: Player fails to reach the finish line within the allotted time limit for race levels (boss levels typically have no time limit, relying solely on health).\n\nUpon losing, the game transitions to the `GAME_OVER` state.",
  "scoring_system": "A prominent scoring system is implemented to encourage skillful play and replayability.\n- **Display**: The current score is prominently displayed in the top-left corner of the screen, formatted as 'SCORE: [value]'.\n- **Actions Giving Points**:\n    - **Rival Overtake**: +10 points for each rival car successfully overtaken without collision.\n    - **Drift Bonus**: +20 points for initiating and maintaining a successful drift for a short duration (e.g., >0.5 seconds).\n    - **Airtime Bonus**: +50 points for successfully navigating a ramp and achieving airtime.\n    - **Level Completion**: +100 points for completing a race level.\n    - **Boss Defeat**: +500 points for defeating a boss car.\n    - **Time Bonus**: At level completion, remaining time (if applicable) * 5 points.\n    - **Health Bonus**: At level completion, remaining health * 2 points.\n- **Bonus Points/Multipliers**:\n    - **Drift Chain Multiplier**: Successfully performing consecutive drifts (without collision or long gaps) increases a multiplier (e.g., x1, x2, x3...). Points gained from subsequent drifts are multiplied. Resets on collision or significant pause in drifting.\n    - **No-Collision Bonus**: Completing an entire level without any collisions with rivals or obstacles awards a x1.5 multiplier to the base level completion points.\n- **High Score System**: The highest score achieved across all play sessions is tracked and displayed on a dedicated 'HIGHSCORES' screen (accessible from the Main Menu), stored locally using `localStorage`.\n- **Score Affects Gameplay**: The score itself primarily serves as a metric for player achievement and high score tracking. It does not directly affect cash earnings or car performance during gameplay. Cash earned (separate from score) is used for upgrades.",
  "level_system": "The game features a progression of levels with increasing difficulty and unique challenges.\n- **Level Indicator**: The current level number is displayed in the top-right corner as 'LEVEL: [Current Level Number]'.\n- **Advancement**: Players advance to the next level by successfully completing the current level's win conditions (reaching the finish line/defeating boss with health > 0).\n- **Transition**: Upon successful completion, a 'LEVEL COMPLETE!' or 'BOSS DEFEATED!' screen appears, showing level score, cash earned, and a 'NEXT LEVEL' button.\n\nHere are 4 progressively difficult levels:\n\n**1. Level 1: \"Suburban Sprint\"**\n    - **Theme**: Sunny, quiet suburban road.\n    - **Length**: Short race (approx. 60-second target completion time).\n    - **Obstacles**: Sparse and simple. Primarily small potholes (stationary), wide single barriers across one lane, very gentle ramps for minimal airtime. Obstacles are predictable and easily avoidable.\n    - **Rivals**: 2-3 slow-moving rival cars with predictable, straight-line paths, rarely changing lanes.\n    - **Speed**: Player's base speed is low. Max speed is low, allowing for easy maneuvering.\n    - **Difficulty**: Easy. Focus on basic steering and acceleration.\n\n**2. Level 2: \"City Rush\"**\n    - **Theme**: Bustling city streets at dusk.\n    - **Length**: Medium race (approx. 90-second target completion time).\n    - **Obstacles**: Moderate density. Includes more frequent potholes, multi-lane barriers, some oil slicks requiring lane changes, and ramps that require slightly more precise angle to hit for a boost. Introduces slow-moving dynamic obstacles (e.g., construction cones shifting between lanes).\n    - **Rivals**: 4-5 moderately faster rival cars, occasionally changing lanes or trying to block. More aggressive AI.\n    - **Speed**: Player's base speed is increased. Max speed is medium, requiring quicker reflexes.\n    - **Difficulty**: Medium. Introduces more complex obstacle patterns and slightly more competitive rivals. Good for practicing drifts.\n\n**3. Level 3: \"Mountain Pass Ascent\"**\n    - **Theme**: Winding mountain road with scenic overlooks and precarious cliff edges (visuals only, no fall-off hazards).\n    - **Length**: Long race (approx. 120-second target completion time).\n    - **Obstacles**: High density and complexity. Features frequent tight chicanes (multiple barriers in quick succession), longer stretches of oil slicks, multiple ramps often leading into or out of tunnels (visual overlays). Dynamic obstacles are faster and more numerous. Some sections may have 'mud patches' (visual+gameplay: driving on them temporarily reduces speed and handling).\n    - **Rivals**: 6-8 fast and aggressive rival cars, constantly jockeying for position, weaving across multiple lanes, and attempting to cut off the player.\n    - **Speed**: Player's base speed is significantly increased. Max speed is high, demanding constant attention.\n    - **Difficulty**: Hard. Requires advanced maneuvering, strategic drifting, and quick decision-making to avoid heavy collision damage.\n\n**4. Level 4: \"Boss Battle: The Enforcer\"**\n    - **Theme**: An enclosed, futuristic arena track with stark lighting.\n    - **Length**: No time limit, instead the boss car has a health bar that must be depleted.\n    - **Obstacles**: Minimal regular obstacles. The primary 'obstacle' is the **Boss Car**, 'The Enforcer', which is larger and more armored. It actively rams the player, leaves behind temporary hazards (e.g., spiked traps, explosive mines) on the track, or emits damaging energy fields.\n    - **Rivals**: Only the Boss Car. No other standard rivals.\n    - **Speed**: Player speed is determined by upgrades. The boss car has varied speed, powerful acceleration, and unique attack patterns requiring dodging and strategic attack (e.g., hitting its exposed rear after it attempts a ram).\n    - **Difficulty**: Very Hard. This level is a direct combat encounter requiring pattern recognition, precise dodging, and sustained damage output.",
  "simplifications": "To ensure feasibility and focus for an HTML5 implementation using p5.js and/or Matter.js, the following simplifications from the original concept are made:\n- **Game Scope**: Reduced the scale from '33 unique levels across 7 distinct locations, culminating in battles against 8 unique boss cars' to 4 specific levels (3 race, 1 boss) across 3 distinct visual themes for this initial specification. This provides a complete, playable game loop while allowing for future expansion.\n- **Car Collectibles & Garage**: Instead of '7 collectible sports cars', the game starts with a single player car. The upgrade system (Engine, Acceleration, Handling) is retained for this single car. Cosmetic customizations (paint jobs/accessories) are simplified to purely visual changes without gameplay impact.\n- **Mobile Controls**: The original touch-based 'finger drag/lift' controls are fully adapted to a keyboard-centric scheme for browser play.\n- **3D Perspective**: The game shifts from a 3D perspective to a top-down 2D, vertically scrolling view, which is more manageable for p5.js/Matter.js and aligns with the 'tight controls and satisfying drift feedback' conversion note.\n- **Physics Complexity**: While Matter.js can be used for car-to-car and car-to-obstacle collision detection and basic responses, full-fledged vehicle dynamics (suspension, tire friction models beyond simple drift effects) are abstracted. Player car movement (acceleration, steering across lanes) is handled primarily via p5.js calculations for predictable arcade control.\n- **Damage/Health System**: Simplified to a single health bar for the player car and boss cars. More complex damage models (e.g., individual car part damage) are omitted.\n- **Multiplayer**: The game is strictly single-player, as multiplayer implementation is outside the scope of this detailed concept and adds significant complexity for a mobile-to-HTML5 conversion.",
  "implementation_notes": "The game should be implemented primarily using p5.js for rendering, input handling, and overall game state management. Matter.js is highly recommended for handling rigid body physics for car-to-car collisions, car-to-obstacle collisions, and potentially for managing static track elements, providing more natural impact responses than purely algorithmic collision detection. The top-down, vertically scrolling perspective will require careful management of asset positions relative to the camera's scroll. The score display ('SCORE: [value]') must be persistently visible in the top-left during the `PLAYING` state. Similarly, the level indicator ('LEVEL: [number]') must be visible in the top-right. Level progression is strictly linear, requiring the player to complete the current level's objectives to unlock and proceed to the next. High scores and car upgrade/cash data should be saved and loaded using `localStorage` to ensure persistence across browser sessions."
}
</task>
<example_html>
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
  <head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8" />
    <style>
      html, body { margin: 0; padding: 0; height: 100%; overflow: hidden; background: #222; }
      body { display: flex; flex-direction: column; justify-content: center; align-items: center; }
      canvas { border: 1px solid #333; width: 600px !important; height: 400px !important; }
      .control-buttons { display: flex; gap: 10px; margin-bottom: 10px; flex-wrap: wrap; justify-content: center; }
      .control-button { padding: 8px 16px; cursor: pointer; background: #444; color: #fff; border: none; border-radius: 4px; }
      .control-button.active { background: #007bff; }
    </style>
  </head>
  <body>
    <div style="text-align: center; margin-bottom: 20px;">
      <h1 id="gameTitle" style="color: #fff; font-family: Arial, sans-serif; margin-bottom: 10px;">{game_title}</h1>
      <div class="control-buttons">
        <button id="humanModeBtn" class="control-button active" onclick="window.setControlMode('HUMAN')">Human Mode</button>
        <button id="test_1_ModeBtn" class="control-button" onclick="window.setControlMode('TEST_1')">TEST (Basic testing)</button>
        <button id="test_2_ModeBtn" class="control-button" onclick="window.setControlMode('TEST_2')">TEST (Win)</button>
      </div>
      <p id="gameDescription" style="color: #ccc; font-family: Arial, sans-serif; max-width: 600px; margin: 0 auto 20px auto; line-height: 1.4;">{game_description}</p>
      <p id="gameControls" style="color: #ccc; font-family: Arial, sans-serif; max-width: 600px; margin: 0 auto 20px auto; line-height: 1.4;">{game_controls}</p>
    </div>
    <script src="https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/p5.js/1.4.0/p5.js"></script>
    <script src="https://unpkg.com/p5.collide2d@0.7.3/p5.collide2d.js"></script>
    <script type="module" src="game.js"></script>
  </body>
</html>

Race through increasingly challenging tracks in this top-down arcade racer! Navigate your car through traffic, avoid obstacles, and master the art of drifting. Progress through suburban streets, busy city roads, and treacherous mountain passes before facing off against the ultimate boss car. Collect cash to upgrade your vehicle's engine, acceleration, and handling. Complete each level without losing all your health to advance. Can you defeat The Enforcer and claim victory? Arrow Up / W: Accelerate (hold to maintain speed) Arrow Left / A: Steer Left Arrow Right / D: Steer Right Space: Hard Brake / Drift (hold while turning for drift bonus) Enter: Start Game Esc: Pause / Unpause R: Restart (return to start screen) // globals.js - Global constants and game state export const CANVAS_WIDTH = 600; export const CANVAS_HEIGHT = 400; export const TARGET_FPS = 60; // Game phases export const PHASE_START = "START"; export const PHASE_PLAYING = "PLAYING"; export const PHASE_PAUSED = "PAUSED"; export const PHASE_GAME_OVER_WIN = "GAME_OVER_WIN"; Pixel Drift Race

Race through increasingly challenging tracks in this top-down arcade racer! Navigate your car through traffic, avoid obstacles, and master the art of drifting. Progress through suburban streets, busy city roads, and treacherous mountain passes before facing off against the ultimate boss car. Collect cash to upgrade your vehicle's engine, acceleration, and handling. Complete each level without losing all your health to advance. Can you defeat The Enforcer and claim victory?

Arrow Up / W: Accelerate (hold to maintain speed)
Arrow Left / A: Steer Left
Arrow Right / D: Steer Right
Space: Hard Brake / Drift (hold while turning for drift bonus)
Enter: Start Game
Esc: Pause / Unpause
R: Restart (return to start screen)
